---
title: "Fisher Information: How Much a Sample Can Tell You"
subtitle: "Core Sample · Statistical Inference"
---

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# A consistent, restrained visual style for every figure in this article.
plt.rcParams.update({
    "figure.figsize": (6.2, 4.0),
    "figure.dpi": 140,
    "axes.spines.top": False,
    "axes.spines.right": False,
    "axes.grid": True,
    "grid.alpha": 0.25,
    "font.size": 11,
    "axes.titlesize": 12,
    "lines.linewidth": 2.0,
})

DATA   = "#2b3a67"     # data / observed points
CURVE  = "#c1121f"     # the log-likelihood / score curve
ACCENT = "#0a7d4d"     # highlights / the true value / the bound
GREY   = "#6c757d"
FILL   = "#9fb3d1"     # shaded densities / bands
```

> **Where this sits in the track.** This is the third article of the **Likelihood Spine**.
> It assumes [INFR-B1 *The Likelihood*], which built the log-likelihood $\ell(\theta)$ and
> its slope, the **score** $U(\theta)=\partial_\theta\ell(\theta)$, and [INFR-B2 *Maximum
> Likelihood*], which found the estimator $\hat\theta$ by solving the score equation
> $U(\hat\theta)=0$. From [INFR-A1 *The Estimator as a Random Variable*] it carries the one
> idea everything here rests on: an estimator is a random variable with a **variance**, and
> a smaller variance means a more precise estimate. From the Probability Foundations track
> it borrows expectation, variance, and the rule for **differentiating under the integral
> sign** (stated and motivated below, [PROB-09]), plus the Cauchy–Schwarz inequality
> ([PROB-07]). Nothing else above the Calculus-I floor is assumed. This article installs the
> object that measures **how much a sample can tell you about a parameter** — the **Fisher
> information** — and uses it to prove a hard floor, the **Cramér–Rao bound**, on the
> precision of *any* unbiased estimator. It sets up [INFR-B4] (the MLE's asymptotic variance
> *is* the reciprocal of this information), [INFR-C3] (Wald intervals are built from it), and
> [INFR-D3] (the score test reads it off the curvature).

## Abstract {.unnumbered}

The likelihood ranks parameter values; its peak gives an estimate. But a peak can be a
needle-sharp spike or a broad, useless mound, and the difference is everything: a sharp
peak means the data have *pinned the parameter down*, a flat one means they have barely
spoken. **Fisher information** $I(\theta)$ is the number that measures that sharpness. We
define it two equivalent ways — as the **variance of the score** and as the **negative
expected curvature** of the log-likelihood — and prove the two definitions agree, using
only the rule for differentiating under the integral and the fact that a density integrates
to one. We prove the score has **mean zero** at the truth (so its variance, the
information, is just its mean square), prove that information **adds over independent
observations** ($I_n(\theta) = n\,I_1(\theta)$ — the precise sense in which more data is
more information), and derive the **Cramér–Rao lower bound**: no unbiased estimator can
have variance below $1/I_n(\theta)$. An estimator that hits the floor is called
**efficient**. We compute $I(\theta)$ end-to-end for the Bernoulli, Poisson, and Normal
models, and walk to the edge where the bound goes silent — biased estimators, boundary
parameters, and finite samples. The single claim the article defends is that *Fisher
information converts the curvature of the log-likelihood into a universal speed limit on
precision, and tells you, before you even pick an estimator, the best any estimator could
possibly do.*

# The Core {#sec-core}

::: {.callout-important title="The Core"}
The **Fisher information** $I(\theta)$ is how sharply the data pin down the parameter — the
**curvature of the log-likelihood at its peak**, averaged over the data the model would
produce. Two equivalent formulas, proved equal below:
$$
I(\theta) \;=\; \underbrace{\operatorname{Var}_\theta\!\bigl[\,U(\theta)\,\bigr]}_{\text{spread of the score}}
\;=\; \underbrace{-\,\mathbb{E}_\theta\!\Bigl[\tfrac{\partial^2}{\partial\theta^2}\ell(\theta)\Bigr]}_{\text{average downward curvature}},
\qquad U(\theta)=\frac{\partial}{\partial\theta}\ell(\theta).
$$
It has three properties that make it the master quantity of estimation. **(1)** The score
has mean zero, so the information is the score's mean square. **(2)** It **adds** over
independent observations: $n$ i.i.d. points carry $I_n(\theta) = n\,I_1(\theta)$ — twice
the data, twice the information, *exactly*. **(3)** It is a **floor on precision**: the
**Cramér–Rao bound** says every unbiased estimator $\hat\theta$ obeys
$$
\operatorname{Var}(\hat\theta) \;\ge\; \frac{1}{I_n(\theta)} \;=\; \frac{1}{n\,I_1(\theta)}.
$$
An estimator that attains this bound is **efficient** — no unbiased competitor can beat it.
A sharp log-likelihood (large $I$) admits a precise estimate; a flat one (small $I$) forbids
it.
:::

Everything below orbits that box. The phrase to hold is *curvature is information*. A
function that bends sharply downward at its maximum is one where moving away from the peak
costs you a lot of fit — so the data strongly prefer the peak, and an estimator can place it
confidently. A function that is nearly flat on top barely distinguishes the peak from its
neighbors — the data are nearly indifferent, and any estimate is fragile. Fisher information
is that bend, made into a number, and the Cramér–Rao bound is the statement that the bend
*is* the precision: you cannot estimate more sharply than the log-likelihood curves.

# Why we are doing this: the problem that forced the idea {#sec-why}

Keep one question in view for the whole article: **how much can a sample tell you about a
parameter, and what is the best precision any estimator could hope for?** Every definition
and derivation below answers a piece of that. If a step feels like notation for its own
sake, return here.

## The problem: a peak is not enough

By [INFR-B2] we can find the maximum-likelihood estimate $\hat\theta$ — the parameter value
that best explains the data. But [INFR-A1] taught us that $\hat\theta$ is a *random
variable*: collect a different sample and you get a different estimate. A point estimate
without a sense of its spread is half an answer. The natural next question — *how much does
$\hat\theta$ wobble from sample to sample?* — is the variance of the estimator, and we need
to know it before we can attach an honest interval to our estimate ([INFR-C3]) or test a
claim about $\theta$ ([INFR-D3]).

[INFR-B1] already showed us, in a picture, where the answer must hide. @fig-edge-flat there
contrasted two coins that produced the *same* $\hat p = 0.75$: with $n=200$ flips the
log-likelihood was a tight spike, with $n=4$ a broad mound. Same peak, wildly different
trustworthiness. The location of the peak — the estimate — was identical; what differed was
the **width** of the peak, equivalently how sharply the log-likelihood **curves** away from
the top. The information we are after is not in *where* the peak is but in *how sharp* it
is. We need to turn that sharpness into a number.

## The deeper problem: is there a best possible estimator?

There is a second, more ambitious question lurking. For any parameter we can usually invent
*many* estimators — the sample mean, a trimmed mean, the median, a weird weighted thing. We
can compare any two by their variances. But is there a *limit*? Is there a precision so good
that **no** unbiased estimator, however clever, can beat it? If such a floor exists, it would
let us judge an estimator against perfection rather than against the competition we happened
to think of — and it would tell us when to stop searching, because we have hit the wall.

That floor exists, and it is the reciprocal of the Fisher information. This is one of the
most beautiful facts in statistics: the *same* quantity that measures the curvature of the
log-likelihood — a property of the **model**, computable before any estimator is chosen —
turns out to be the exact speed limit on **every** estimator. The model itself tells you the
best you could ever do. Deriving why is the spine of this article.

## A word on who built it

The object is named for **R. A. Fisher**, who in the 1920s — the same papers that named the
likelihood ([INFR-B1]) — introduced "information" as the amount of knowledge a sample yields
about a parameter and recognized the curvature of the log-likelihood as its measure. The
lower bound was found independently by **Harald Cramér** (1946) and **C. R. Rao** (1945),
and a closely related form by **Maurice Fréchet** and **Georges Darmois**; the statistics
world settled on "Cramér–Rao." The *why* threaded through everything below is theirs: turn
the geometry of the likelihood — how it bends — into the arithmetic of precision.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Intuitively, what does Fisher information measure?"* It measures how much the data, on
average, will sharpen your knowledge of the parameter — equivalently, how sharply the
log-likelihood is curved at its peak. The one-sentence test answer: *high Fisher information
means the data strongly distinguish nearby parameter values (a sharp likelihood, a precise
estimate); low Fisher information means they barely do (a flat likelihood, a fragile
estimate).* If pressed for the connection to estimation, add: its reciprocal is a lower
bound on the variance of any unbiased estimator (Cramér–Rao), and it is the asymptotic
variance of the maximum-likelihood estimator scaled by $n$ ([INFR-B4]). The interviewer is
checking that you tie *information* to *curvature* to *precision*, not that you recite a
formula.
:::

# Intuition before symbols {#sec-intuition}

Before any formula, picture two log-likelihood curves over the same parameter, peaking at the
same place. One is a steep, narrow valley flipped upside-down — a sharp dome. The other is a
gentle, wide hill. @fig-intuition draws both for the coin of [INFR-B1].

```{python}
#| label: fig-intuition
#| fig-cap: 'Curvature is information. Two log-likelihoods for the bias of a coin, both peaking at $\hat p = 0.6$, shifted to share a peak height of zero. The sharp curve (red, from $n=100$ flips) bends steeply away from the top: moving the bias even a little costs a lot of fit, so the data strongly prefer values near $0.6$ — the estimate is precise. The flat curve (grey, from $n=10$) barely bends: a wide band of biases explains the data almost equally, so the estimate is loose. Fisher information is the downward curvature at the peak, and the figure shows it controlling the *width* of the region the data find plausible.'
p_grid = np.linspace(0.2, 0.95, 600)

def shifted_loglik(n, phat):
    k = phat * n
    ell = k * np.log(p_grid) + (n - k) * np.log(1 - p_grid)
    return ell - ell.max()

ell_sharp = shifted_loglik(100, 0.6)
ell_flat = shifted_loglik(10, 0.6)

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.plot(p_grid, ell_sharp, color=CURVE, label=r"sharp: $n=100$ (large $I$)")
ax.plot(p_grid, ell_flat, color=GREY, label=r"flat: $n=10$ (small $I$)")
ax.axvline(0.6, color=ACCENT, ls="--", lw=1.5, label=r"shared peak $\hat p=0.6$")
ax.axhline(0, color=GREY, lw=0.8, alpha=0.6)
ax.set_ylim(-6, 0.4)
ax.set_xlabel("bias $p$")
ax.set_ylabel(r"log-likelihood (peak shifted to 0)")
ax.set_title("A sharp peak carries more information")
ax.legend(loc="lower center", fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
```

How do we turn "sharpness" into a number? In Calc 1, the sharpness of a curve at a peak is
its **second derivative**. At a maximum the first derivative (the slope, the score) is zero
and the second derivative is *negative* — the curve is concave down. The more negative the
second derivative, the more sharply the curve bends away from the top. So a first guess at
"information" is "$-\ell''$ at the peak," and that guess is almost exactly right. Two
refinements turn it into Fisher information, and the formal section earns each:

- **We average over data, not fix one sample.** The curvature $\ell''$ depends on the
  particular sample we drew. Fisher information is the curvature the model would produce *on
  average* — an **expectation** over the data-generating distribution. This makes it a
  property of the model and the true parameter, not of one dataset, which is exactly what we
  need to compare estimators *before* seeing data.
- **It equals the spread of the score.** Astonishingly, that same average curvature also
  equals the **variance of the slope** (the score) — a fact with no Calc-1 analogue, which
  we prove in full. It is the bridge from "how the curve bends" to "how the estimate wobbles."

# The formal development {#sec-formal}

Throughout, the data are $x_1,\dots,x_n$ from a model with mass/density $p(x\mid\theta)$ and
a *single* real parameter $\theta$ (the multi-parameter version is a matrix; we flag it at
the end). We write $\ell(\theta) = \sum_i \log p(x_i\mid\theta)$ for the log-likelihood and
$U(\theta) = \ell'(\theta)$ for the score, both from [INFR-B1]. The subscript on
$\mathbb{E}_\theta$ and $\operatorname{Var}_\theta$ is a reminder that the expectation is
taken over data drawn from the model **at parameter $\theta$** — the average is over possible
datasets, with $\theta$ playing the role of the truth.

## The one imported tool: differentiating under the integral {#sec-dui}

Every proof below leans on a single move from above the Calc-1 floor, so we state it plainly,
motivate it, and then use it in the open. A density integrates to one for *every* parameter
value:
$$
\int p(x\mid\theta)\,dx = 1 \qquad \text{for all } \theta
$$
(sum instead of integral for discrete models; everything below works identically with
$\sum_x$ in place of $\int dx$). The right-hand side, $1$, does not depend on $\theta$, so
its derivative with respect to $\theta$ is $0$. We want to also differentiate the *left*-hand
side, and the question is whether we may move the derivative **inside** the integral:
$$
\frac{d}{d\theta}\int p(x\mid\theta)\,dx
\;\stackrel{?}{=}\;
\int \frac{\partial}{\partial\theta}\,p(x\mid\theta)\,dx.
$$
Swapping the order of "differentiate" and "integrate" is not free in general — it can fail
when the region where $p>0$ itself moves with $\theta$, or when the integrand misbehaves at
the limits. But for the standard families we use (Bernoulli, Poisson, Normal, and the broad
class called **regular** models), it is valid, and the technical hypotheses that license it
are exactly the **regularity conditions** of maximum-likelihood theory.

::: {.callout-note title="What we are importing, and where it is built"}
The theorem that licenses $\frac{d}{d\theta}\int f(x,\theta)\,dx = \int
\partial_\theta f(x,\theta)\,dx$ — sometimes called the **Leibniz rule for parameter
differentiation** — is built in the Probability Foundations track ([PROB-09]); it requires
that $f$ and $\partial_\theta f$ be suitably bounded and that the **support** $\{x :
p(x\mid\theta) > 0\}$ not depend on $\theta$. We import only its statement. The fixed-support
clause is not fine print: it is *precisely* what fails for the uniform $[0,\theta]$ model,
whose support $[0,\theta]$ moves with the parameter — and that is exactly why Fisher
information and the Cramér–Rao bound break for it, as we show honestly in @sec-edge. When the
support is fixed, the swap is legal and the whole theory runs.
:::

With the swap in hand, differentiate the normalization $\int p\,dx = 1$ both sides:
$$
0 = \frac{d}{d\theta}\!\int p(x\mid\theta)\,dx
= \int \frac{\partial p(x\mid\theta)}{\partial\theta}\,dx.
$$ {#eq-dui-once}
This little identity — *the integral of the parameter-derivative of the density is zero* —
is the seed from which both the mean-zero score and the two-formula equivalence grow. We
will also need it differentiated a second time; we do that when we get there.

## The score has mean zero {#sec-score-mean}

The score $U(\theta) = \ell'(\theta) = \sum_i \frac{\partial}{\partial\theta}\log
p(x_i\mid\theta)$ is, for fixed data, a number, but as a function of the *random* data it is
itself a random variable. Its expectation, taken at the true parameter, is exactly zero —
the foundation of everything.

::: {#lem-score-mean}
**Lemma (the score is mean-zero).** Under the regularity conditions of @sec-dui, the score
of a single observation has expectation zero at the true parameter:
$$
\mathbb{E}_\theta\!\left[\frac{\partial}{\partial\theta}\log p(X\mid\theta)\right] = 0.
$$
Consequently the total score $U(\theta)=\sum_{i=1}^n \frac{\partial}{\partial\theta}\log
p(X_i\mid\theta)$ also has mean zero, $\mathbb{E}_\theta[U(\theta)] = 0$.
:::

::: {.proof}
Start from the single-observation score and rewrite the log-derivative. The chain rule for
the logarithm gives $\frac{\partial}{\partial\theta}\log p = \frac{1}{p}\cdot
\frac{\partial p}{\partial\theta}$ (the derivative of $\log u$ is $u'/u$), so
$$
\frac{\partial}{\partial\theta}\log p(x\mid\theta)
= \frac{1}{p(x\mid\theta)}\,\frac{\partial p(x\mid\theta)}{\partial\theta}.
$$
This identity — score $=$ (derivative of density) over (density) — is worth committing to
memory; it is the hinge of nearly every proof in this article. Now take its expectation over
$X$ at parameter $\theta$. By definition of expectation, $\mathbb{E}_\theta[g(X)] = \int
g(x)\,p(x\mid\theta)\,dx$, so
$$
\mathbb{E}_\theta\!\left[\frac{\partial}{\partial\theta}\log p(X\mid\theta)\right]
= \int \frac{1}{p(x\mid\theta)}\,\frac{\partial p(x\mid\theta)}{\partial\theta}
\;\cdot\; p(x\mid\theta)\,dx.
$$
The factor $\frac{1}{p}$ from the score and the factor $p$ from the expectation **cancel**
exactly (this is why we needed the score in the $p'/p$ form — the $p$ from the averaging
kills the denominator):
$$
= \int \frac{\partial p(x\mid\theta)}{\partial\theta}\,dx = 0,
$$
where the final equality is precisely @eq-dui-once, the differentiated normalization. So the
single-observation score has mean zero. For the total score over an independent sample, the
expectation of a sum is the sum of the expectations, and each term is zero, so
$\mathbb{E}_\theta[U(\theta)] = \sum_{i=1}^n 0 = 0$. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

Read what this says. At the true parameter, the slope of the log-likelihood is *on average*
zero — the data have no systematic preference to push the parameter up or down from the
truth. The estimate $\hat\theta$ (the root of the score) is therefore aimed at $\theta$, not
biased away from it. But "on average zero" does not mean "always zero": for any particular
sample the score is some nonzero number, and how much it *scatters* around zero is exactly
what determines how much $\hat\theta$ scatters around $\theta$. That scatter is the
variance of the score — which we are about to name the Fisher information.

## Fisher information, definition one: the variance of the score {#sec-defn-var}

::: {#def-fisher-var}
**Definition (Fisher information, score-variance form).** The **Fisher information** in a
single observation is the variance of the single-observation score at the true parameter:
$$
I_1(\theta) \;=\; \operatorname{Var}_\theta\!\left[\frac{\partial}{\partial\theta}\log p(X\mid\theta)\right]
\;=\; \mathbb{E}_\theta\!\left[\left(\frac{\partial}{\partial\theta}\log p(X\mid\theta)\right)^{\!2}\right].
$$
The second equality holds because the score has mean zero (@lem-score-mean): for any
mean-zero random variable $W$, $\operatorname{Var}(W) = \mathbb{E}[W^2] - (\mathbb{E}[W])^2 =
\mathbb{E}[W^2] - 0 = \mathbb{E}[W^2]$.
:::

This is the definition that explains *why information bounds precision*: the score is the
data's "pull" on the parameter, and the more that pull varies from sample to sample, the
more the data discriminate between parameter values — and, as the Cramér–Rao proof will show,
the more sharply an estimator can locate $\theta$. But this form, an expected square of a
first derivative, is often awkward to compute. The second definition, in terms of curvature,
is usually easier, and the fact that the two agree is one of the most useful identities in
statistics.

## Fisher information, definition two: the expected curvature {#sec-defn-curv}

::: {#thm-information-equivalence}
**Theorem (the two forms of Fisher information agree).** Under the regularity conditions of
@sec-dui, the variance of the score equals the negative expected second derivative of the
log-density:
$$
I_1(\theta)
= \mathbb{E}_\theta\!\left[\left(\frac{\partial}{\partial\theta}\log p(X\mid\theta)\right)^{\!2}\right]
= -\,\mathbb{E}_\theta\!\left[\frac{\partial^2}{\partial\theta^2}\log p(X\mid\theta)\right].
$$
:::

::: {.proof}
We differentiate the identity @eq-dui-once **a second time** and read the equivalence off
the result. The cleanest route differentiates the mean-zero-score statement itself. We
proved
$$
\int \frac{\partial}{\partial\theta}\log p(x\mid\theta)\;\cdot\; p(x\mid\theta)\,dx = 0
$$
(this is @lem-score-mean written as an integral, before the cancellation). The left side is
a function of $\theta$ that is identically zero, so its derivative with respect to $\theta$
is also zero. Differentiate under the integral sign (@sec-dui) and apply the **product rule**
to the integrand, which is a product of two $\theta$-dependent factors,
$\underbrace{\frac{\partial}{\partial\theta}\log p}_{A}\cdot\underbrace{p}_{B}$:
$$
0 = \frac{d}{d\theta}\int A\,B\,dx
= \int \left(\frac{\partial A}{\partial\theta}\,B + A\,\frac{\partial B}{\partial\theta}\right)dx.
$$
Take the two pieces in turn. The first factor's derivative is
$$
\frac{\partial A}{\partial\theta}
= \frac{\partial}{\partial\theta}\!\left(\frac{\partial}{\partial\theta}\log p\right)
= \frac{\partial^2}{\partial\theta^2}\log p,
$$
the second derivative of the log-density. The second piece needs $\frac{\partial B}{\partial
\theta} = \frac{\partial p}{\partial\theta}$, and here we re-use the score identity from
@lem-score-mean in reverse: $\frac{\partial p}{\partial\theta} = p\cdot
\frac{\partial}{\partial\theta}\log p$ (just $\frac{\partial}{\partial\theta}\log p =
\frac1p\frac{\partial p}{\partial\theta}$ multiplied through by $p$). So
$$
A\,\frac{\partial B}{\partial\theta}
= \frac{\partial}{\partial\theta}\log p \;\cdot\; p\,\frac{\partial}{\partial\theta}\log p
= p\left(\frac{\partial}{\partial\theta}\log p\right)^{\!2}.
$$
Substitute both pieces back:
$$
0 = \int \left[\frac{\partial^2}{\partial\theta^2}\log p\right] p\,dx
\;+\; \int \left(\frac{\partial}{\partial\theta}\log p\right)^{\!2} p\,dx.
$$
Each integral is now an expectation against $p(x\mid\theta)\,dx$ — that is the definition
$\mathbb{E}_\theta[g] = \int g\,p\,dx$ again. The first is
$\mathbb{E}_\theta\!\left[\frac{\partial^2}{\partial\theta^2}\log p\right]$ and the second is
$\mathbb{E}_\theta\!\left[\left(\frac{\partial}{\partial\theta}\log p\right)^2\right]$, so
$$
0 = \mathbb{E}_\theta\!\left[\frac{\partial^2}{\partial\theta^2}\log p(X\mid\theta)\right]
\;+\; \mathbb{E}_\theta\!\left[\left(\frac{\partial}{\partial\theta}\log p(X\mid\theta)\right)^{\!2}\right].
$$
Move the first term to the other side. The right-hand expectation is exactly $I_1(\theta)$
by @def-fisher-var, so
$$
I_1(\theta) = \mathbb{E}_\theta\!\left[\left(\frac{\partial}{\partial\theta}\log p\right)^{\!2}\right]
= -\,\mathbb{E}_\theta\!\left[\frac{\partial^2}{\partial\theta^2}\log p(X\mid\theta)\right],
$$
which is the claimed equality. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

The minus sign is doing real work, and it is not a sign convention to memorize: the log-
likelihood is concave near its peak, so its second derivative is *negative*; negating it
makes the information *positive*, as a variance must be. The two readings now sit side by
side. The variance-of-score form (@def-fisher-var) tells you *why* information is precision —
it is the spread of the data's pull. The curvature form (@thm-information-equivalence) tells
you *how to compute it* — differentiate the log-likelihood twice and take an expectation,
which is almost always the easier arithmetic. We will use the curvature form for every
worked example.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Fisher information has two definitions — variance of the score, and negative expected
second derivative. Are they always equal, and which do you use?"* They are equal **under the
regularity conditions** (fixed support, legal differentiation under the integral); the proof
is the one above — differentiate the normalization twice. In practice you compute with the
**curvature form** $-\mathbb{E}[\ell'']$ because second derivatives of a log-density are
usually cleaner than the expected square of a first derivative. You should also be ready for
the catch: the equality *fails* exactly when the regularity conditions fail — most
famously the uniform $[0,\theta]$, whose support moves with the parameter — and there the
whole Fisher-information / Cramér–Rao apparatus does not apply. Naming that failure case
unprompted is what separates a memorized answer from an understood one.
:::

## Information adds: $I_n(\theta) = n\,I_1(\theta)$ {#sec-additivity}

We have so far worked with a single observation. The information in the *whole sample* is
what bounds the estimate, and for independent data it is simply $n$ copies of the
single-observation information.

::: {#thm-additivity}
**Theorem (information adds over independent observations).** For an independent sample
$X_1,\dots,X_n$ from the same model, the Fisher information in the full sample is
$$
I_n(\theta) = n\,I_1(\theta).
$$
:::

::: {.proof}
Use the variance-of-score form. The total score is the sum of the single-observation scores,
$U(\theta) = \sum_{i=1}^n U_i(\theta)$ where $U_i(\theta) = \frac{\partial}{\partial\theta}
\log p(X_i\mid\theta)$, because the log-likelihood is a sum (the additivity bought by the
logarithm in [INFR-B1]) and the derivative of a sum is the sum of the derivatives. The
Fisher information in the sample is the variance of this total score:
$$
I_n(\theta) = \operatorname{Var}_\theta[U(\theta)] = \operatorname{Var}_\theta\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^n U_i(\theta)\right].
$$
Now invoke the rule for the variance of a sum of **independent** random variables: variance
distributes across an independent sum with no covariance terms,
$\operatorname{Var}(\sum_i W_i) = \sum_i \operatorname{Var}(W_i)$ (the cross terms
$\operatorname{Cov}(W_i, W_j)$ for $i\neq j$ all vanish under independence — built in
[INFR-A1] and [PROB-06]). The $X_i$ are independent, so the $U_i$ are independent, hence
$$
I_n(\theta) = \sum_{i=1}^n \operatorname{Var}_\theta[U_i(\theta)].
$$
Finally, the observations are identically distributed, so each
$\operatorname{Var}_\theta[U_i(\theta)]$ is the *same* number — the single-observation
information $I_1(\theta)$ of @def-fisher-var. Summing $n$ identical copies,
$$
I_n(\theta) = \sum_{i=1}^n I_1(\theta) = n\,I_1(\theta). \qquad\blacksquare
$$
:::

This is the rigorous version of the slogan *more data is more information* — and it is sharper
than the slogan, because it says information grows **exactly linearly**: a hundred
observations carry exactly one hundred times the information of one, not more, not less.
Through the Cramér–Rao bound (next), this linear growth becomes the familiar
$\operatorname{Var}(\hat\theta) \ge \frac{1}{n I_1(\theta)}$ — variance shrinking like
$1/n$, standard error like $1/\sqrt{n}$, the universal rate we first met in the central limit
theorem ([INFR-A2]). The two ideas are the same fact seen from two sides: averaging $n$
observations sharpens the estimate at rate $\sqrt{n}$ because each independent observation
adds one fixed unit of information.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Why does the standard error of an estimate shrink like $1/\sqrt{n}$ and not, say, $1/n$?"*
Because information adds linearly. Each independent observation contributes a fixed amount
$I_1(\theta)$ of Fisher information, so $n$ of them give $n\,I_1(\theta)$; the best possible
variance is the reciprocal, $\frac{1}{n I_1(\theta)}$, which falls like $1/n$; and the
standard error is the square root of the variance, which falls like $1/\sqrt{n}$. To halve
your standard error you must **quadruple** your sample — the practical cost of the
square-root law, and the reason large precision gains get expensive fast.
:::

## The Cramér–Rao lower bound {#sec-crb}

We now prove the centerpiece: Fisher information is a floor on the precision of every
unbiased estimator. We state it for an estimator of $\theta$ itself; the general version for
estimating a function $g(\theta)$ falls out of the same proof and we record it after.

::: {#thm-crb}
**Theorem (Cramér–Rao lower bound).** Let $\hat\theta = \hat\theta(X_1,\dots,X_n)$ be any
**unbiased** estimator of $\theta$, meaning $\mathbb{E}_\theta[\hat\theta] = \theta$ for all
$\theta$, in a regular model (@sec-dui). Then
$$
\operatorname{Var}_\theta(\hat\theta) \;\ge\; \frac{1}{I_n(\theta)} \;=\; \frac{1}{n\,I_1(\theta)}.
$$
:::

::: {.proof}
The whole proof is one application of the **Cauchy–Schwarz inequality** to two random
variables: the estimator $\hat\theta$ and the total score $U(\theta)$. We first compute their
covariance, then square the Cauchy–Schwarz inequality, then read off the bound.

**Step 1 — the covariance of the estimator and the score is exactly 1.** Recall the score
$U(\theta) = \frac{\partial}{\partial\theta}\ell(\theta) = \frac1{p}\frac{\partial p}{\partial
\theta}$ summed over the sample, where now $p(x\mid\theta) = \prod_i p(x_i\mid\theta)$ is the
*joint* density of the whole sample (the score of the sample is the log-derivative of the
joint density — same identity, applied to the product). The unbiasedness assumption says,
written as an integral over the sample space with $\mathbf{x} = (x_1,\dots,x_n)$,
$$
\mathbb{E}_\theta[\hat\theta] = \int \hat\theta(\mathbf{x})\,p(\mathbf{x}\mid\theta)\,d\mathbf{x} = \theta
\qquad \text{for all }\theta.
$$
Differentiate both sides with respect to $\theta$. The right side gives $\frac{d}{d\theta}
\theta = 1$. The left side we differentiate under the integral (@sec-dui), and since
$\hat\theta(\mathbf{x})$ does not depend on $\theta$ (it is a fixed function of the data) the
derivative lands only on $p$:
$$
1 = \int \hat\theta(\mathbf{x})\,\frac{\partial p(\mathbf{x}\mid\theta)}{\partial\theta}\,d\mathbf{x}.
$$
Now use the score identity $\frac{\partial p}{\partial\theta} = p\cdot
\frac{\partial}{\partial\theta}\log p = p\,U(\theta)$ to convert the bare density-derivative
into the score times the density:
$$
1 = \int \hat\theta(\mathbf{x})\,U(\theta)\,p(\mathbf{x}\mid\theta)\,d\mathbf{x}
= \mathbb{E}_\theta\!\bigl[\hat\theta\,U(\theta)\bigr].
$$
Since the score has mean zero (@lem-score-mean, $\mathbb{E}_\theta[U]=0$), the right side is
exactly the **covariance** of $\hat\theta$ and $U$:
$$
\operatorname{Cov}_\theta(\hat\theta, U)
= \mathbb{E}_\theta[\hat\theta\,U] - \mathbb{E}_\theta[\hat\theta]\,\mathbb{E}_\theta[U]
= \mathbb{E}_\theta[\hat\theta\,U] - \theta\cdot 0
= \mathbb{E}_\theta[\hat\theta\,U] = 1.
$$
So the estimator and the score covary perfectly to the value $1$ — a clean, dimensionless
hook the inequality will pull on.

**Step 2 — Cauchy–Schwarz.** For any two random variables $A$ and $B$, the Cauchy–Schwarz
inequality for covariances states
$$
\bigl[\operatorname{Cov}(A,B)\bigr]^2 \;\le\; \operatorname{Var}(A)\,\operatorname{Var}(B).
$$
(This is the probabilistic form of the familiar vector inequality $\langle a,b\rangle^2 \le
\|a\|^2\|b\|^2$, with "inner product" $= $ covariance; it is built in [PROB-07], and its
content is just that a correlation coefficient lies in $[-1,1]$.) Apply it with $A=\hat\theta$
and $B=U(\theta)$:
$$
\bigl[\operatorname{Cov}_\theta(\hat\theta, U)\bigr]^2 \;\le\; \operatorname{Var}_\theta(\hat\theta)\,\operatorname{Var}_\theta(U).
$$

**Step 3 — substitute the two quantities we know.** The left side is $1^2 = 1$ by Step 1.
The variance of the score on the right is, by definition, the Fisher information of the whole
sample: $\operatorname{Var}_\theta(U) = I_n(\theta)$ (this is @def-fisher-var applied to the
sample score; equivalently @thm-additivity says it equals $n I_1(\theta)$). So
$$
1 \;\le\; \operatorname{Var}_\theta(\hat\theta)\,I_n(\theta).
$$
Divide both sides by $I_n(\theta)$, which is positive (a variance, and nonzero for any model
that carries information), to isolate the estimator's variance:
$$
\operatorname{Var}_\theta(\hat\theta) \;\ge\; \frac{1}{I_n(\theta)} = \frac{1}{n\,I_1(\theta)}. \qquad\blacksquare
$$
:::

That is the speed limit. Notice what went into it: the model's regularity (to differentiate
under the integral), the estimator's unbiasedness (to make the covariance equal exactly $1$),
and one inequality. No assumption about *which* estimator — the bound holds for the sample
mean, the median, and every clever scheme anyone will ever invent. The Fisher information,
computed from the model alone, caps them all.

::: {#def-efficiency}
**Definition (efficiency).** An unbiased estimator that attains the Cramér–Rao bound,
$\operatorname{Var}_\theta(\hat\theta) = 1/I_n(\theta)$, is called **efficient**. More
generally, the **efficiency** of an unbiased estimator is the ratio
$$
\text{eff}(\hat\theta) = \frac{1/I_n(\theta)}{\operatorname{Var}_\theta(\hat\theta)} \in (0,1],
$$
the fraction of the available information it manages to use; efficiency $1$ means it wastes
nothing.
:::

When does an estimator hit the floor? The single inequality in the proof was Cauchy–Schwarz
(Step 2), so the bound is attained exactly when *that* step is an equality, and Cauchy–Schwarz
is an equality precisely when its two random variables are **linearly dependent** — the
probabilistic version of "$\langle a,b\rangle^2 = \|a\|^2\|b\|^2$ iff $b$ is a scalar multiple
of $a$," the condition that the correlation between them is $\pm 1$ (this dependence-at-equality
clause is part of the Cauchy–Schwarz statement we imported from [PROB-07]). Here the two
variables are $\hat\theta$ and the score $U(\theta)$, so equality requires that, for each
$\theta$, the score be an affine function of the estimator,
$$
U(\theta) = a(\theta)\,\hat\theta + b(\theta),
$$
for some coefficients $a(\theta), b(\theta)$ that may depend on $\theta$ but not on the data.
We can pin down both coefficients from facts already proved, with no new machinery. Take the
expectation at $\theta$: the left side is $\mathbb{E}_\theta[U] = 0$ (@lem-score-mean) and the
right side is $a(\theta)\,\mathbb{E}_\theta[\hat\theta] + b(\theta) = a(\theta)\,\theta +
b(\theta)$ (unbiasedness), so $b(\theta) = -a(\theta)\,\theta$ and the relation becomes
$U(\theta) = a(\theta)(\hat\theta - \theta)$. To find $a(\theta)$, multiply this by $\hat\theta$
and take expectations: the left side is $\mathbb{E}_\theta[\hat\theta\,U] = 1$ (Step 1 of the
proof), and the right side is $a(\theta)\,\mathbb{E}_\theta[\hat\theta(\hat\theta-\theta)] =
a(\theta)\,\operatorname{Cov}_\theta(\hat\theta,\hat\theta) = a(\theta)\operatorname{Var}_\theta(\hat\theta)$
(using $\mathbb{E}_\theta[\hat\theta]=\theta$ so that $\mathbb{E}_\theta[\hat\theta(\hat\theta-\theta)]$
is exactly the variance). Hence $a(\theta) = 1/\operatorname{Var}_\theta(\hat\theta)$, and at the
floor $\operatorname{Var}_\theta(\hat\theta) = 1/I_n(\theta)$, so $a(\theta) = I_n(\theta)$. The
attainment condition is therefore
$$
U(\theta) = I_n(\theta)\,(\hat\theta - \theta):
$$
the score must be a fixed multiple of the estimation error — equivalently, the estimator must be
recoverable *linearly from the score*. This linear-in-the-score structure is exactly the
defining feature of the **exponential families** (which include the Bernoulli, Poisson, and
Normal we are about to compute), and it is why the sample mean is efficient for those. For
models outside that class the score is a *nonlinear* function of any unbiased estimator, so
Cauchy–Schwarz is strict and no unbiased estimator reaches the floor at finite $n$ — but the
maximum-likelihood estimator reaches it *asymptotically*, as $n\to\infty$, which is the deep
payoff worked out in [INFR-B4].

::: {.callout-note title="The bound for a function of the parameter"}
The same proof, with one extra derivative, handles an unbiased estimator $T$ of a smooth
function $g(\theta)$ (e.g. estimating the variance $\sigma^2$, or the odds $p/(1-p)$).
Differentiating $\mathbb{E}_\theta[T] = g(\theta)$ gives $\operatorname{Cov}_\theta(T, U) =
g'(\theta)$ in Step 1 instead of $1$, and Cauchy–Schwarz then yields
$$
\operatorname{Var}_\theta(T) \;\ge\; \frac{[\,g'(\theta)\,]^2}{I_n(\theta)}.
$$
The squared derivative $[g'(\theta)]^2$ is the **delta-method** factor — the local
sensitivity of $g$ to $\theta$ — which reappears as the engine of [INFR-B4]'s treatment of
functions of the MLE. We record it here because it is the form a regression coefficient's or
a transformed parameter's bound actually takes in practice.
:::

# Worked examples {#sec-examples}

We now compute Fisher information end-to-end for the three workhorse models, by hand from the
curvature form $I_1(\theta) = -\mathbb{E}_\theta[\ell_1''(\theta)]$ where $\ell_1$ is the
single-observation log-likelihood. In each case we then check the Cramér–Rao bound against
the estimator we already know from [INFR-B2], and confirm the arithmetic against simulation.

## Example 1 — Bernoulli: the information in a coin flip {#sec-ex-bernoulli}

A single Bernoulli observation $X\in\{0,1\}$ with $P(X=1)=p$ has log-density (from
[INFR-B1])
$$
\ell_1(p) = x\log p + (1-x)\log(1-p).
$$
Differentiate once for the score, using $\frac{d}{dp}\log p = \frac1p$ and
$\frac{d}{dp}\log(1-p) = \frac{-1}{1-p}$:
$$
\frac{\partial \ell_1}{\partial p} = \frac{x}{p} - \frac{1-x}{1-p}.
$$
Differentiate a second time. The derivative of $\frac{x}{p} = x p^{-1}$ is $-x p^{-2} =
-\frac{x}{p^2}$; the derivative of $-\frac{1-x}{1-p} = -(1-x)(1-p)^{-1}$ is
$-(1-x)(1-p)^{-2}\cdot(-1)\cdot(-1) = -\frac{1-x}{(1-p)^2}$ (one minus from the existing sign,
one from the chain rule on $1-p$, which combine to a single minus):
$$
\frac{\partial^2 \ell_1}{\partial p^2} = -\frac{x}{p^2} - \frac{1-x}{(1-p)^2}.
$$
Now take the negative expectation. The only random thing is $x$, and $\mathbb{E}_p[X] = p$,
so $\mathbb{E}_p[1-X] = 1-p$:
$$
I_1(p) = -\,\mathbb{E}_p\!\left[\frac{\partial^2 \ell_1}{\partial p^2}\right]
= \mathbb{E}_p\!\left[\frac{X}{p^2} + \frac{1-X}{(1-p)^2}\right]
= \frac{p}{p^2} + \frac{1-p}{(1-p)^2}
= \frac{1}{p} + \frac{1}{1-p}.
$$
Put the two fractions over a common denominator $p(1-p)$:
$$
\boxed{\;I_1(p) = \frac{1}{p} + \frac{1}{1-p} = \frac{(1-p) + p}{p(1-p)} = \frac{1}{p(1-p)}.\;}
$$ {#eq-info-bernoulli}
Read this curve. Information is **largest near $p=0$ and $p=1$** — when the coin is nearly
deterministic, a single flip is very telling — and **smallest at $p=\tfrac12$**, where the
coin is maximally random and one flip says the least. By @thm-additivity, $n$ flips carry
$I_n(p) = \frac{n}{p(1-p)}$, so the Cramér–Rao bound on any unbiased estimator of $p$ is
$\operatorname{Var}(\hat p) \ge \frac{p(1-p)}{n}$. The MLE $\hat p = \bar X = k/n$ from
[INFR-B2] has variance $\operatorname{Var}(\bar X) = \frac{\operatorname{Var}(X)}{n} =
\frac{p(1-p)}{n}$ — it **hits the bound exactly**, so the sample proportion is efficient.
@fig-info-bernoulli draws the information curve and confirms the bound by simulation.

```{python}
#| label: fig-info-bernoulli
#| fig-cap: 'Fisher information for the Bernoulli, and the Cramér–Rao bound in action. **Left:** the per-observation information $I_1(p)=1/[p(1-p)]$ (red) blows up as the coin nears certainty ($p\to0$ or $1$) and bottoms out at the maximally-random $p=\tfrac12$ — one flip of a fair coin is the least informative flip there is. **Right:** for $p=0.3$, $n=40$, the empirical variance of the sample proportion $\hat p=\bar X$ across 20000 simulated samples (blue) sits right on the Cramér–Rao floor $p(1-p)/n$ (green dashed): the MLE is efficient — it extracts all the information the bound allows.'
p_grid = np.linspace(0.02, 0.98, 500)
I1 = 1.0 / (p_grid * (1 - p_grid))

p_true, n_obs = 0.3, 40
rng = np.random.default_rng(0)
phats = rng.binomial(n_obs, p_true, size=20000) / n_obs
emp_var = phats.var()
crb = p_true * (1 - p_true) / n_obs

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8.8, 3.7))
axL.plot(p_grid, I1, color=CURVE)
axL.axvline(0.5, color=GREY, ls=":", lw=1.4)
axL.annotate("least informative\nat $p=0.5$", (0.5, 1.0/0.25),
             textcoords="offset points", xytext=(8, 10), fontsize=8, color=GREY)
axL.set_xlabel("bias $p$"); axL.set_ylabel(r"$I_1(p)=1/[p(1-p)]$")
axL.set_ylim(0, 30)
axL.set_title("Information per flip")

axR.hist(phats, bins=40, density=True, color=FILL, alpha=0.7)
axR.axvline(p_true, color=ACCENT, ls="--", lw=1.6, label=fr"true $p={p_true}$")
axR.set_xlabel(r"sample proportion $\hat p$"); axR.set_ylabel("density")
axR.set_title("Sampling distribution of $\\hat p$")
axR.legend(loc="upper right", fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
print(f"empirical Var(p_hat) = {emp_var:.6f}")
print(f"Cramer-Rao bound     = {crb:.6f}   (p(1-p)/n)")
print(f"ratio (efficiency)   = {crb/emp_var:.4f}   (~1 means MLE attains the bound)")
```

## Example 2 — Poisson: the information in a count {#sec-ex-poisson}

A single Poisson count $X\in\{0,1,2,\dots\}$ with rate $\lambda$ has log-density (dropping
the $-\log x!$ constant, which has zero derivative in $\lambda$)
$$
\ell_1(\lambda) = x\log\lambda - \lambda - \log x!.
$$
Score (first derivative; $\frac{d}{d\lambda}\log\lambda = \frac1\lambda$, the derivative of
$-\lambda$ is $-1$, the constant drops):
$$
\frac{\partial \ell_1}{\partial\lambda} = \frac{x}{\lambda} - 1.
$$
Second derivative ($\frac{x}{\lambda} = x\lambda^{-1}$ differentiates to $-x\lambda^{-2}$;
the $-1$ is constant):
$$
\frac{\partial^2 \ell_1}{\partial\lambda^2} = -\frac{x}{\lambda^2}.
$$
Negative expectation, using the Poisson mean $\mathbb{E}_\lambda[X] = \lambda$:
$$
\boxed{\;I_1(\lambda) = -\,\mathbb{E}_\lambda\!\left[-\frac{X}{\lambda^2}\right]
= \frac{\mathbb{E}_\lambda[X]}{\lambda^2} = \frac{\lambda}{\lambda^2} = \frac{1}{\lambda}.\;}
$$ {#eq-info-poisson}
Information per observation is $1/\lambda$: a **rare** process (small $\lambda$) is *more*
informative per count about its rate, because the counts are tightly concentrated, while a
high-rate process scatters its counts widely and each one says less. By additivity $I_n =
n/\lambda$, so the bound is $\operatorname{Var}(\hat\lambda) \ge \lambda/n$. The MLE
$\hat\lambda = \bar X$ has variance $\frac{\operatorname{Var}(X)}{n} = \frac{\lambda}{n}$
(the Poisson variance equals its mean $\lambda$) — again **exactly the bound**, so $\bar X$
is efficient for the Poisson rate.

```{python}
#| label: fig-info-poisson
#| fig-cap: 'The Cramér–Rao bound for the Poisson rate, verified by simulation. For each true rate $\lambda$ we draw 20000 samples of $n=25$ counts, estimate the rate by the MLE $\hat\lambda=\bar X$, and plot the empirical variance (blue dots) against the Cramér–Rao floor $\lambda/n$ (green line). The dots lie on the line across the whole range: the sample mean attains the bound for every rate. The variance *grows* with $\lambda$ because higher-rate processes carry less information per count ($I_1=1/\lambda$).'
n_obs = 25
lams = np.linspace(0.5, 8.0, 16)
rng = np.random.default_rng(1)
emp = []
for lam in lams:
    lamhats = rng.poisson(lam, size=(20000, n_obs)).mean(axis=1)
    emp.append(lamhats.var())
emp = np.array(emp)
bound = lams / n_obs

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.plot(lams, bound, color=ACCENT, label=r"Cramér–Rao bound $\lambda/n$")
ax.scatter(lams, emp, color=DATA, s=35, zorder=3, label=r"empirical $\mathrm{Var}(\hat\lambda)$")
ax.set_xlabel(r"true rate $\lambda$"); ax.set_ylabel(r"variance of $\hat\lambda$")
ax.set_title("MLE attains the bound for every rate")
ax.legend(loc="upper left", framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
print(f"max relative gap between empirical and bound: {np.max(np.abs(emp-bound)/bound):.3f}")
```

## Example 3 — Normal: information about the mean, and about the variance {#sec-ex-normal}

The Normal carries two parameters, and computing the information about each separately is
both the most useful case in practice and the cleanest illustration that information depends
on which parameter you are after.

**Information about the mean $\mu$ (variance $\sigma^2$ known).** The single-observation
log-density is
$$
\ell_1(\mu) = -\tfrac12\log(2\pi\sigma^2) - \frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}.
$$
Score (only the second term depends on $\mu$; chain rule on $(x-\mu)^2$ gives $2(x-\mu)\cdot
(-1)$, so the $\frac1{2\sigma^2}$ and the $2$ combine):
$$
\frac{\partial\ell_1}{\partial\mu} = \frac{x-\mu}{\sigma^2}.
$$
Second derivative (the derivative of $\frac{x-\mu}{\sigma^2}$ with respect to $\mu$ is
$-\frac{1}{\sigma^2}$, a constant):
$$
\frac{\partial^2\ell_1}{\partial\mu^2} = -\frac{1}{\sigma^2}.
$$
This second derivative is already a constant — it does not depend on the random $x$ at all —
so the expectation is trivial:
$$
\boxed{\;I_1(\mu) = -\,\mathbb{E}_\mu\!\left[-\frac{1}{\sigma^2}\right] = \frac{1}{\sigma^2}.\;}
$$ {#eq-info-normal-mu}
Information about the mean is $1/\sigma^2$ per observation: the **noisier** the measurements
(larger $\sigma^2$), the **less** each tells you about the center — exactly the intuition you
would demand. By additivity $I_n(\mu) = n/\sigma^2$, so the bound is
$\operatorname{Var}(\hat\mu) \ge \sigma^2/n$. The MLE $\hat\mu = \bar X$ has variance exactly
$\sigma^2/n$ — efficient again, and this is the precise sense in which the sample mean is the
best possible unbiased estimator of a Normal mean.

**Information about the variance $\sigma^2$ (mean $\mu$ known).** Write $v=\sigma^2$ to
reduce clutter. The log-density as a function of $v$ is
$$
\ell_1(v) = -\tfrac12\log(2\pi) - \tfrac12\log v - \frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2v}.
$$
Score (the derivative of $-\tfrac12\log v$ is $-\frac{1}{2v}$; the derivative of
$-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2v} = -\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2}v^{-1}$ is $+\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2}v^{-2} =
\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2v^2}$):
$$
\frac{\partial\ell_1}{\partial v} = -\frac{1}{2v} + \frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2v^2}.
$$
Second derivative (the derivative of $-\frac1{2v} = -\tfrac12 v^{-1}$ is $\tfrac12 v^{-2} =
\frac{1}{2v^2}$; the derivative of $\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2}v^{-2}$ is $-2\cdot\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2}
v^{-3} = -\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{v^3}$):
$$
\frac{\partial^2\ell_1}{\partial v^2} = \frac{1}{2v^2} - \frac{(x-\mu)^2}{v^3}.
$$
Negative expectation. The only random piece is $(x-\mu)^2$, and since $\mu$ is the true mean,
$\mathbb{E}_v[(X-\mu)^2] = \operatorname{Var}(X) = v$ by definition of the variance:
$$
I_1(v) = -\,\mathbb{E}_v\!\left[\frac{1}{2v^2} - \frac{(X-\mu)^2}{v^3}\right]
= -\frac{1}{2v^2} + \frac{\mathbb{E}_v[(X-\mu)^2]}{v^3}
= -\frac{1}{2v^2} + \frac{v}{v^3}.
$$
The last term is $\frac{v}{v^3} = \frac{1}{v^2}$, so
$$
\boxed{\;I_1(\sigma^2) = -\frac{1}{2v^2} + \frac{1}{v^2} = \frac{1}{2v^2} = \frac{1}{2\sigma^4}.\;}
$$ {#eq-info-normal-var}
The information about the variance is $\frac{1}{2\sigma^4}$ per observation, so the
Cramér–Rao bound is $\operatorname{Var}(\widehat{\sigma^2}) \ge \frac{2\sigma^4}{n}$.
@fig-info-normal checks both bounds against simulation, and exposes an honest subtlety in the
variance case.

```{python}
#| label: fig-info-normal
#| fig-cap: 'Both Normal information bounds, verified by simulation ($\mu=0$, $\sigma^2=4$, $n=30$, 30000 samples). **Left:** the sample mean $\bar X$ — its empirical variance (blue) sits exactly on the floor $\sigma^2/n$ (green), efficient. **Right:** for the variance, the *unbiased* estimator $\frac{1}{n-1}\sum(X_i-\bar X)^2$ (blue) sits just above the Cramér–Rao floor $2\sigma^4/n$ (green) — close but not equal, because no unbiased estimator of $\sigma^2$ attains the bound at finite $n$. The MLE divisor-$n$ version is biased, so the bound does not even apply to it; this is the honest boundary of ''efficiency.'''
mu_true, v_true, n_obs = 0.0, 4.0, 30
rng = np.random.default_rng(2)
samples = rng.normal(mu_true, np.sqrt(v_true), size=(30000, n_obs))

xbar = samples.mean(axis=1)
var_unbiased = samples.var(axis=1, ddof=1)   # divisor n-1, unbiased

emp_var_mean = xbar.var()
crb_mean = v_true / n_obs

emp_var_var = var_unbiased.var()
crb_var = 2 * v_true**2 / n_obs

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8.8, 3.7))
axL.hist(xbar, bins=45, density=True, color=FILL, alpha=0.7)
axL.axvline(mu_true, color=ACCENT, ls="--", lw=1.5)
axL.set_xlabel(r"$\bar X$"); axL.set_ylabel("density")
axL.set_title("Mean: hits the bound")

axR.hist(var_unbiased, bins=45, density=True, color=FILL, alpha=0.7)
axR.axvline(v_true, color=ACCENT, ls="--", lw=1.5, label=r"true $\sigma^2=4$")
axR.set_xlabel(r"unbiased $\widehat{\sigma^2}$"); axR.set_ylabel("density")
axR.set_title("Variance: just above the bound")
axR.legend(loc="upper right", fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
print(f"MEAN:     empirical Var = {emp_var_mean:.5f}   bound sigma^2/n   = {crb_mean:.5f}   eff = {crb_mean/emp_var_mean:.3f}")
print(f"VARIANCE: empirical Var = {emp_var_var:.5f}   bound 2 sigma^4/n = {crb_var:.5f}   eff = {crb_var/emp_var_var:.3f}")
```

The mean estimator is efficient to within simulation noise; the unbiased variance estimator
is *close* to its bound but does not reach it (efficiency a bit below $1$). That gap is not a
mistake — it is the honest content of @def-efficiency: outside the special structure that
made the mean efficient, no unbiased estimator attains the floor at finite $n$, though the
MLE closes the gap as $n\to\infty$ ([INFR-B4]). We will say more about this in @sec-edge.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"For a Normal sample, is the sample mean the best estimator of $\mu$? Prove it."* Yes, among
unbiased estimators, and the proof is two lines of this article: the Fisher information about
$\mu$ is $I_n(\mu) = n/\sigma^2$ (@eq-info-normal-mu times $n$), so the Cramér–Rao bound is
$\operatorname{Var}(\hat\mu) \ge \sigma^2/n$; the sample mean $\bar X$ has variance exactly
$\sigma^2/n$, so it attains the bound and is efficient. A sharp follow-up is *"what about the
median?"* — the median is also unbiased for a symmetric Normal but has variance $\approx
\frac{\pi}{2}\cdot\frac{\sigma^2}{n} \approx 1.57\,\sigma^2/n$, an efficiency of about
$64\%$. It wastes more than a third of the information, which is the precise, quantitative
sense in which the mean beats the median for Normal data — and why you would still prefer the
median when the data are *not* Normal, where it is far more robust (a trade the bound, which
assumes the model, cannot see).
:::

# Failure modes and the edge of applicability {#sec-edge}

The Cramér–Rao bound is one of the cleanest results in statistics, which makes it dangerous:
it is easy to quote where it does not hold. Here are the four boundaries, stated honestly.

## The bound only constrains *unbiased* estimators {#sec-edge-bias}

The theorem assumed $\mathbb{E}_\theta[\hat\theta] = \theta$. Drop that, and the bound says
*nothing* — a biased estimator can have variance **below** $1/I_n(\theta)$, sometimes far
below. This is not a loophole; it is a genuine and useful phenomenon. The MLE of the Normal
variance, $\hat\sigma^2 = \frac1n\sum(X_i-\bar X)^2$, is biased low, and its *mean squared
error* can beat the unbiased $\frac{1}{n-1}$ version. The entire enterprise of **regularized
/ shrinkage** estimators (ridge regression, the James–Stein estimator) deliberately accepts
bias to buy a large variance reduction, landing below the unbiased floor in total error.
@fig-edge-bias shows a shrinkage estimator of a Normal mean doing exactly this.

```{python}
#| label: fig-edge-bias
#| fig-cap: 'Bias can beat the bound. Estimating a Normal mean $\mu=1$ from $n=8$ noisy observations ($\sigma=3$). The unbiased sample mean $\bar X$ (blue) has variance on the Cramér–Rao floor $\sigma^2/n$. A shrinkage estimator $\hat\mu_c = c\,\bar X$ with $c=0.7$ (orange) is *biased* toward zero, so the bound does not apply to it — and indeed its mean squared error (variance plus squared bias) dips **below** the unbiased floor here. The Cramér–Rao bound caps unbiased estimators only; trading a little bias for a lot of variance is a real and widely-used escape.'
mu_true, sigma, n_obs = 1.0, 3.0, 8
rng = np.random.default_rng(4)
xbar = rng.normal(mu_true, sigma, size=(60000, n_obs)).mean(axis=1)

cs = np.linspace(0.0, 1.3, 60)
mse = []
for c in cs:
    est = c * xbar
    mse.append(np.mean((est - mu_true) ** 2))
mse = np.array(mse)
crb = sigma**2 / n_obs

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.plot(cs, mse, color="#d97706", label=r"MSE of $\hat\mu_c = c\,\bar X$")
ax.axhline(crb, color=ACCENT, ls="--", lw=1.6, label=r"unbiased floor $\sigma^2/n$")
ax.axvline(1.0, color=GREY, ls=":", lw=1.4)
ax.annotate("$c=1$:\nsample mean", (1.0, crb*1.05), fontsize=8, color=GREY)
best_c = cs[np.argmin(mse)]
ax.scatter([best_c], [mse.min()], color=CURVE, s=50, zorder=4,
           label=f"best shrinkage $c={best_c:.2f}$")
ax.set_xlabel(r"shrinkage factor $c$"); ax.set_ylabel("mean squared error")
ax.set_title("A biased estimator dips below the unbiased bound")
ax.legend(loc="upper center", fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
print(f"unbiased floor sigma^2/n   = {crb:.4f}")
print(f"min MSE over c             = {mse.min():.4f}  at c = {best_c:.3f}")
```

## Moving support: the bound does not apply at all {#sec-edge-support}

The regularity conditions of @sec-dui demanded a **fixed support**. The textbook violator is
the uniform model $X\sim\text{Uniform}[0,\theta]$, whose density $\frac1\theta$ lives on
$[0,\theta]$ — a region that *moves* with $\theta$. There you cannot differentiate under the
integral, the two information formulas disagree, and the Cramér–Rao bound is simply false:
the MLE $\hat\theta = \max_i X_i$ has variance shrinking like $1/n^2$, *faster* than any
$1/n$ bound would permit. This is the same model that breaks the score-equation approach to
the MLE in [INFR-B2], and for the same reason — the maximum is on the boundary, where
calculus has no purchase. The lesson: **before quoting Cramér–Rao, check that the support
does not depend on the parameter.**

```{python}
#| label: fig-edge-support
#| fig-cap: 'Where the bound is simply false. For $X\sim\text{Uniform}[0,\theta]$ with $\theta=1$, the support $[0,\theta]$ moves with the parameter, so the regularity conditions fail. The MLE $\hat\theta=\max_i X_i$ (blue) has variance that collapses like $1/n^2$ (red dashed) — dramatically faster than the $1/n$ rate (grey dashed) that any Cramér–Rao bound would impose. The Fisher-information machinery does not merely become loose here; it does not apply, and the estimator outruns the ''limit.'''
theta = 1.0
ns = np.array([5, 10, 20, 40, 80, 160, 320])
rng = np.random.default_rng(6)
emp = []
for n_obs in ns:
    mx = rng.uniform(0, theta, size=(40000, n_obs)).max(axis=1)
    emp.append(mx.var())
emp = np.array(emp)

# variance of the max of n Uniform[0,1] is n / [(n+1)^2 (n+2)] ~ 1/n^2
exact = ns / ((ns + 1.0)**2 * (ns + 2.0))

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.loglog(ns, emp, "o", color=DATA, label=r"empirical $\mathrm{Var}(\hat\theta)$")
ax.loglog(ns, exact, color=CURVE, ls="--", label=r"exact $\sim 1/n^2$ rate")
ax.loglog(ns, 1.0 / ns * exact[0] * ns[0], color=GREY, ls=":",
          label=r"a $1/n$ rate (for contrast)")
ax.set_xlabel("sample size $n$"); ax.set_ylabel(r"variance of $\hat\theta$")
ax.set_title("Uniform $[0,\\theta]$: the MLE beats any $1/n$ bound")
ax.legend(loc="lower left", fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
```

## The bound is a *lower* bound, often not attained at finite $n$ {#sec-edge-attain}

Even in a perfectly regular model, the bound says only that you cannot do *better* than
$1/I_n$ — it does not promise an estimator that does *as well*. We saw this with the Normal
variance: the unbiased estimator hovers above its floor at finite $n$. For most models
outside the exponential family, the floor is reached only **asymptotically**, by the MLE, as
$n\to\infty$. So "efficient" is, in general, an *asymptotic* property; reading the
finite-sample bound as a guarantee of achievable precision overstates it. The exact
finite-sample variance of an estimator can sit well above the bound, and the gap is not
necessarily anyone's fault.

## A multi-parameter caveat {#sec-edge-matrix}

With more than one parameter — the realistic case, e.g. $(\mu,\sigma^2)$ together — the
Fisher information becomes a **matrix** $\mathbf{I}(\theta)$ whose $(j,k)$ entry is
$\mathbb{E}[\partial_j\ell\,\partial_k\ell] = -\mathbb{E}[\partial_j\partial_k\ell]$, and the
Cramér–Rao bound becomes a statement about matrices: $\operatorname{Cov}(\hat\theta)
\succeq \mathbf{I}_n^{-1}$ (the difference is positive semidefinite). The bound on a single
component is then $\operatorname{Var}(\hat\theta_j) \ge [\mathbf{I}_n^{-1}]_{jj}$ — the
$j$-th *diagonal of the inverse*, **not** the reciprocal of the $j$-th diagonal. The
distinction matters whenever the parameters are correlated: estimating $\mu$ when $\sigma^2$
is also unknown is no harder than when it is known *only because* the off-diagonal Fisher
information between $\mu$ and $\sigma^2$ happens to be zero for the Normal — a lucky
orthogonality, not a general rule. The matrix machinery, the inverse, and the quadratic forms
it needs are developed where the multi-parameter MLE lives, [INFR-B4]; we flag it here so the
single-parameter formulas above are not over-generalized.

::: {.callout-warning title="Pitfall"}
**Three ways the Cramér–Rao bound is misquoted.** (1) *Applying it to a biased estimator* —
the bound constrains only unbiased estimators; biased estimators routinely have smaller
variance, which is the whole point of shrinkage (@sec-edge-bias). (2) *Quoting it for a
moving-support model* — for the uniform $[0,\theta]$ and its relatives the bound is false,
not just loose (@sec-edge-support). (3) *In the multi-parameter case, using $1/I_{jj}$
instead of $[\mathbf{I}^{-1}]_{jj}$* — the inverse of the matrix, then the diagonal, in that
order; reversing them silently ignores the cost of the nuisance parameters
(@sec-edge-matrix). Each error makes precision look better than it is.
:::

# When Fisher information is the right tool — and when it is not {#sec-applicability}

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"When would you actually reach for Fisher information?"* The honest summary:

- **Reach for it** whenever you have a **regular parametric model** and want to (a) know the
  *best achievable precision* before committing to an estimator, (b) build a standard error
  or **Wald confidence interval** for an MLE — $\hat\theta \pm z\,/\sqrt{I_n(\hat\theta)}$,
  the workhorse of [INFR-C3]; (c) construct a **score test** ([INFR-D3]); or (d) reason about
  the asymptotic variance of the MLE, which *is* $1/I_n$ ([INFR-B4]). It is the single most
  reused quantity in classical inference, and every coefficient standard error a regression
  package prints is a Fisher-information calculation underneath.
- **Reach for something else** when (a) the model is **misspecified** — the information
  computed under the wrong model is wrong, and a *robust* "sandwich" variance is safer; (b)
  the model is **irregular** (moving support, boundary parameter), where the bound does not
  apply and exact or order-statistic arguments are needed; (c) the sample is **small** and
  the asymptotic interpretation of efficiency is shaky — the **bootstrap** ([INFR-E1])
  estimates variance directly without trusting the curvature; or (d) you genuinely want a
  **biased** estimator for lower total error, where the bound is irrelevant by construction.
- **The deepest caveat:** Fisher information is a property of the **model you assumed**. It
  tells you the best precision *if that model is true*. It cannot warn you that the model is
  wrong — that is the job of goodness-of-fit and diagnostics. A confidently small
  Cramér–Rao bound under a misspecified model is precision toward the wrong target.
:::

# How you would actually compute this {#sec-implementation}

The math is primary; this is the secondary, practical note. You rarely compute
$\mathbb{E}[\ell'']$ by hand for a real model. Instead, fitting libraries return the
**observed information** — the *actual* negative second derivative of the log-likelihood at
the MLE, $-\ell''(\hat\theta)$, not its expectation — because at the estimate it is a drop-in
proxy for $I_n(\theta)$ and needs no expectation. `statsmodels` exposes it as the inverse of
the parameter covariance: `result.cov_params()` is $[-\ell''(\hat\theta)]^{-1}$, and the
printed coefficient standard errors are its square-root diagonal. A generic optimizer
(`scipy.optimize.minimize`) returns the inverse Hessian in `result.hess_inv`, which is the
same observed-information inverse. The distinction between the **expected** information
$\mathbb{E}[-\ell'']$ (this article's $I(\theta)$) and the **observed** information
$-\ell''(\hat\theta)$ (what software uses) is real but small for large $n$ — they converge by
the law of large numbers ([INFR-A2]) — and which to prefer is a genuine, debated practical
question that [INFR-B4] takes up. When a package prints a standard error, it has computed an
information and inverted it; you now know which information and why.

# Connections and where to go next {#sec-connections}

::: {.callout-important title="Where you are in the track"}
You arrived from **[INFR-B2 *Maximum Likelihood*]**, which found the estimate $\hat\theta$ as
the root of the score. There we located the *peak* of the log-likelihood; here we measured
its *sharpness*, and turned that sharpness into a hard limit on precision. From the
**Likelihood Spine** the trunk continues:

- **[INFR-B4 *Why Maximum Likelihood Works*]** — the payoff. The MLE's asymptotic
  distribution is $\sqrt{n}(\hat\theta - \theta) \to \mathcal{N}\!\bigl(0,\,I_1(\theta)^{-1}\bigr)$:
  its variance is *exactly* the reciprocal Fisher information of this article, so the MLE is
  **asymptotically efficient** — it attains the Cramér–Rao bound in the limit. The
  delta-method form $[g'(\theta)]^2/I_n$ we recorded here becomes the variance of a function
  of the MLE.
- **[INFR-C3 *Intervals From the Likelihood*]** — the **Wald interval** $\hat\theta \pm
  z\,\sqrt{I_n(\hat\theta)^{-1}}$ turns the information directly into a confidence interval,
  and we will see exactly where it fails (the binomial near $p=0$ or $1$, where
  @eq-info-bernoulli blows up).
- **[INFR-D3 *The Asymptotic Trinity*]** — the **score test** reads the information off the
  curvature at the null value, the dual of the Wald interval; Fisher information is the
  scaling that makes the test statistic chi-square.

It feeds outward too: every standard error the Regression track prints for a coefficient is a
Fisher-information calculation, which is why this track owns the general theory and Regression
links back to it.
:::

# Going deeper {#sec-deeper}

- **The founding sources.** R. A. Fisher, "Theory of Statistical Estimation," *Proc. Camb.
  Phil. Soc.* 22 (1925), where "information" is defined and tied to the curvature of the
  log-likelihood. The bound: C. R. Rao, "Information and the accuracy attainable in the
  estimation of statistical parameters," *Bull. Calcutta Math. Soc.* 37 (1945), and H.
  Cramér, *Mathematical Methods of Statistics* (1946).
- **The information-geometric view.** Fisher information is a Riemannian *metric* on the space
  of parameters — it measures distance between nearby distributions by how distinguishable
  their data are. This is the entry point to **information geometry** (Amari), where the
  curvature of this article becomes literal geometric curvature.
- **Open threads this article deliberately deferred.** *What is the MLE's exact sampling
  distribution, and does it attain the bound?* — asymptotic normality and efficiency,
  [INFR-B4]. *How do we turn $I$ into an interval and a test?* — Wald and score procedures,
  [INFR-C3] and [INFR-D3]. *What is the multi-parameter information matrix, in full?* — the
  Hessian-expectation and its inverse, [INFR-B4]. *What replaces the bound when the model is
  wrong?* — the robust sandwich variance, a thread the testing arc and Regression pick up.
